posts.name: Drew Goin
posts.cooked:
I have read previous posts on several glorious domestically-produced “Sherry” wines* (dry to cream-style) from yesteryear.

Are there any currently bottled examples that you would recommend?

*Please keep in mind that I am not referring to “Port-style” reds or “Late Harvest” Zins.

Hunt Country makes one, but I haven’t had it:

cellartracker.com

N.V. Hunt Country Vineyards Cream Sherry, USA, New York, Finger Lakes -...

Average of 90 points in 3 community wine reviews, professional notes, label images, wine details, and recommendations on when to drink.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: Does Thunderbird qualify? It’s fortified to roughly sherry level.

This is dry rather than cream-style, made from chardonnay, on tasting our first bottle I thought it was excellent:

NV Salinia Chardonnay Wellspring Solera

2/4/2016 - 90 Points
Lightly gray 14k gold. The noise is waxy, wooly white stone and tropical fruit, the palate is like a salty, zingy, mature white Hermitage, exotic and very slightly oily, but intense and refreshing also. The finish is most Sherry-like, with a bitter, smoky edge. The fact that the base grapes are chardonnay perhaps accounts for this wine’s fullish body. Enjoyed with seared sous vide chicken breast.

cellartracker.com

NV Salinia Chardonnay Wellspring Solera, USA, California, Sonoma County,...

Average of 89.7 points in 16 community wine reviews on NV Salinia Chardonnay Wellspring Solera, plus professional notes, label images, wine details, and recommendations on when to drink.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: It’s not sherry, but you might try Raymond Haak’s Texas Madeiras. They’re very impressive.

posts.name: Clayton Wai-Poi
posts.cooked: Not sherry… but in the digestive space, I’m particularly fond of Mathiason’s Vermouth.

posts.name: Ron Slye
posts.cooked: Lullaby here in WA state makes a sherry. I have wanted to try it but have not yet. We generally like her wines, so I am assuming it is a good one.

posts.name: Theodore Anderson
posts.cooked: Kalin.

posts.name: Drew Goin
posts.cooked:
I know that Quady and Sutton Cellars make some Vermouth-type wines, as well as Solera-style wines.

Thanks for the recommendations!
I didn’t know about Washington state’s Lullaby, and I presume that the Matthiason stuff is super hard to get.

I believe that the Cucamonga area offers sweet “Sherry-style” wines, but none are available for shipment to Louisiana.

Any other suggestions?

posts.name: Drew Goin
posts.cooked:
Interesting article from 1993 on domestic “Sherry”:

Los Angeles Times – 9 Sep 93

California's Lost Pyramids

At one time in California, Sherry was king.

posts.name: Nate Simon
posts.cooked: Harbor Mission Del Sol is a great example of domestic sherry-style wine IMP.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
Peter Tryba:

Don’t most Scholium white wines taste like Sherry?

I was just thinking of this…

posts.name: Drew Goin
posts.cooked:
Nate Simon:

Harbor Mission Del Sol is a great example of domestic sherry-style wine IMP.

I heard so many great things about this wine that I secured a bottle of the 1977 Mission del Sol from WineBid a few months ago. Recently, more guarded words about the volatility of certain bottles have been shared.

I have not requested a shipment of my “wins” yet, so I will have to wait to see if my bottle turns out to be delicious or dreadful.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
Drew Goin:

I know that Quady and Sutton Cellars make some Vermouth-type wines, as well as Solera-style wines.

Thanks for the recommendations!
I didn’t know about Washington state’s Lullaby, and I presume that the Matthiason stuff is super hard to get.

I believe that the Cucamonga area offers sweet “Sherry-style” wines, but none are available for shipment to Louisiana.

Any other suggestions?

Why not just buy vin Jaune from Jura or Sherry from Spain? They’ve been doing it a lot longer than the 'Mericans and probably do it better.

posts.name: Fred Daniels
posts.cooked: It’s been a while, but I would try Korbel, the sparkling wine producer. When I visited them in the 1990s, they made a whole range of wines…cab, chard, madeira (type), and a sherry. They were for sale only at the winery, but maybe they have it available via mail order.

posts.name: Jim Cowan
posts.cooked:
In 2014 I made a Fiano sous voile and while there was no Solera involved, the wine has a slight oxidative note. The technique used was similar to the one used in the Jura.
Best, Jim
Stone Hill makes a surprisingly good Cream Sherry in Missouri.
